perturbation.original_text: The rights, promises, duties, and obligations set out herein, and the validity, interpretation, performance, and legal effect of the whole Agreement shall be governed and determined by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor and Ingram mutually agree that Ingram's public disclosure of the Proprietary Information, except pursuant to a confidential disclosure agreement, to any party will release Vendor from the obligation of confidentiality with respect to that portion of the Proprietary Information actually disclosed by Ingram.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to an irrelevant section reduces its prominence and could lead to disputes about which state's laws apply. It would normally be found closer to the end of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. Moving the governing law clause makes it less clear and prominent, potentially contradicting this principle by obscuring the parties' intended choice of law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies California law governs the agreement. The changed text moves this clause to a less prominent place, potentially creating ambiguity regarding the applicable law. California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place of performance or, if no place is indicated, the place where it is made. By moving the governing law clause, the contract introduces a structural flaw, making the intended choice of law less clear, potentially contradicting Section 1646. The placement of the clause is obscured, making it difficult to interpret, as the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction specifies.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees not to communicate, disclose, or otherwise make available all or any part of the Proprietary Information to any third party, including, but not limited to Vendor's parent, subsidiaries, or affiliated companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor agrees not to directly contact, for the purpose of soliciting, or selling Product directly to any customer or dealer listed in the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the non-disclosure agreement to another section would be misleading and thus reduces clarity and enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets. By moving the non-disclosure clause to another section, it could make it harder to enforce and would obfuscate the actual non-disclosure requirements, therefore making it harder to ensure the "trade secret" is reasonably protected.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clear non-disclosure agreement, while the changed text significantly weakens this by only restricting direct contact for soliciting or selling. This substantially reduces the scope of protection for proprietary information. The Law Explanation states that by moving the non-disclosure clause, it could be harder to enforce and would obfuscate the actual non-disclosure requirements, therefore making it harder to ensure the 'trade secret' is reasonably protected. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which the Law Explanation claims would be harder to enforce due to the modified clause. The structural flaw of moving or weakening the non-disclosure clause creates a potential contradiction by diminishing the protection afforded to trade secrets under CUTSA.
